data.title: Investors House Oyj (HEL:INVEST) Will Pay A €0.25 Dividend In Three Days
data.description:
Investors House Oyj (HEL:INVEST) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next three days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Therefore, if you purchase Investors House Oyj's shares on or after the 15th of April, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 23rd of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be €0.25 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed €0.37 to shareholders. Looking at the last 12 months of distributions, Investors House Oyj has a trailing yield of approximately 8.4% on its current stock price of €4.40. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. Investors House Oyj paid out 54% of its earnings to investors last year, a normal payout level for most businesses. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Investors House Oyj generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. It paid out an unsustainably high 295% of its free cash flow as dividends over the past 12 months, which is worrying. It's pretty hard to pay out more than you earn, so we wonder how Investors House Oyj intends to continue funding this dividend, or if it could be forced to cut the payment.

While Investors House Oyj's dividends were covered by the company's reported profits, cash is somewhat more important, so it's not great to see that the company didn't generate enough cash to pay its dividend. Cash is king, as they say, and were Investors House Oyj to repeatedly pay dividends that aren't well covered by cashflow, we would consider this a warning sign.

Check out our latest analysis for Investors House Oyj

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. That's why it's comforting to see Investors House Oyj's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 49% per annum for the past five years. Earnings have been growing quickly, but we're concerned dividend payments consumed most of the company's cash flow over the past year.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. Investors House Oyj has delivered 8.1% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. We're glad to see dividends rising alongside earnings over a number of years, which may be a sign the company intends to share the growth with shareholders.

Final Takeaway

Has Investors House Oyj got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? The best dividend stocks typically boast a long history of growing earnings per share (EPS) via a combination of earnings growth and buybacks. So, you might think that Investors House Oyj buying back stock, growing its EPS, and retaining profits within its business is a good combination. However, we note with some concern that it paid out 295% of its free cash flow last year, which is uncomfortably high and makes us wonder why the company chose to spend even more cash on buybacks. Overall, it's not a bad combination, but we feel that there are likely more attractive dividend prospects out there.

With that being said, if dividends aren't your biggest concern with Investors House Oyj, you should know about the other risks facing this business. Our analysis shows 4 warning signs for Investors House Oyj that we strongly recommend you have a look at before investing in the company.

data.title: Unilever Refocuses On Beauty And Wellness As Foods Joins McCormick
data.description:
• Unilever is combining its Foods business, including Knorr and Hellmann's, with McCormick to create a global flavour group.
  • The deal marks a pivot for Unilever toward beauty, wellbeing, personal care, and home care as its core focus.
  • Alongside the Foods transaction, Unilever has acquired Grüns, a US based supplements brand, to expand its health and wellness exposure.

For investors in LSE:ULVR, this move comes as the shares trade around £43.13, with a 3.4% gain over the past week but double digit declines over the past month, year, and year to date. The combination of the Foods unit with McCormick and the purchase of Grüns significantly reshapes where Unilever’s revenues may come from over time, with less reliance on traditional pantry brands.

These portfolio changes point to a clearer focus on categories tied to beauty, wellbeing, and household products, as well as science led supplements. For investors, the key questions are how efficiently Unilever executes this shift and how the market chooses to value a more focused, premium oriented consumer business.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Unilever by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Unilever.

We've flagged 2 risks for Unilever. See which could impact your investment.

The McCormick combination and the Grüns purchase together give you a clearer picture of where Unilever wants its growth engines to sit. The Reverse Morris Trust deal values Unilever Foods at an EV/Sales multiple of 3.6x and 13.8x EV/EBITDA, with Unilever and its shareholders set to own 65% of the combined flavour group and receive US$15.7b in cash. Management has flagged that the cash is earmarked to cover separation costs, reduce leverage to around 2.0x net debt to EBITDA, and fund a planned €6,000m share buyback program between 2026 and 2029. At the same time, bolt on deals like Grüns and a stated focus on “super growth assets” in the US and India indicate a tilt toward premium, science led health and wellness brands. For you as an investor, the key trade off is swapping a diversified, pantry heavy portfolio for a more concentrated pure play in Beauty, Wellbeing, Personal Care and Home Care, while still retaining a sizeable equity interest in a global flavour group that competes with peers such as Nestlé and Kraft Heinz.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Unilever Narrative

  • The Foods separation and shift toward science led supplements directly align with the narrative of focusing on premium, higher margin beauty and wellbeing categories supported by bolt on acquisitions.
  • Relying more on health and wellness brands and emerging market premiumisation could challenge the narrative if competition from private label and local brands limits pricing power or slows expected margin improvement.
  • The long term equity stake in the combined McCormick Foods group, and the specific US and India acquisition focus, may not be fully reflected in existing narrative assumptions about category mix and geographic exposure.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Unilever to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk around separating a €39,000m revenue Foods business and integrating with McCormick while also reshaping the remaining portfolio could create operational disruption or cost overruns.
  • ⚠️ A more concentrated focus on beauty, personal care and health products increases exposure to competition from players such as Procter & Gamble, L’Oréal and local digital brands, which could pressure volumes or pricing.
  • 🎁 Retaining 65% of the combined Foods and McCormick entity and receiving US$15.7b in cash provides both ongoing exposure to a large flavour platform and financial flexibility for debt reduction and share buybacks.
  • 🎁 Selective bolt on deals in premium, digitally native wellness and personal care brands in the US and India can deepen exposure to faster growing channels and segments that management has identified as priorities.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how quickly Unilever progresses towards closing the McCormick transaction, the level and timing of the US$600m annual cost synergies targeted by year three, and any updates on regulatory approvals. It is also worth tracking how management paces the planned €6,000m buyback program and whether further US or India focused acquisitions in health and wellbeing are announced. Shareholders may want to watch for signs that the remaining pure play Home and Personal Care portfolio is gaining traction in premium and ecommerce channels, as well as how the combined flavour business performs once Unilever stops consolidating its Foods earnings.

data.title: Is Snap (SNAP) A Potential Opportunity After A 40% Year To Date Share Price Slide
data.description:
• Wondering whether Snap at around US$4.82 is a bargain or a value trap? This article breaks down what the current share price might be implying about the company.
  • The stock has been volatile recently, with a 4.1% move over the last week, a small 0.8% slip over 30 days, and much steeper share price declines of 40.7% year to date and 39.4% over the past year.
  • These moves come against a backdrop of ongoing attention on Snap's user engagement, product direction, and its position in the broader social media and advertising space. Together, these themes help frame why sentiment on the stock can shift quickly and why investors are closely watching how the business is perceived.
  • Snap currently has a valuation score of 4 out of 6, suggesting that on several metrics the shares may be pricing in a different outlook than some models imply. Next up is a look at how traditional valuation approaches stack up against a more complete way of thinking about value later in the article.

Approach 1: Snap Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to the present.

For Snap, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $422.8 million. Analysts provide detailed forecasts for the next few years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out, with projected free cash flow of $1.63b in 2030 according to the model.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of around $15.05 per share. Compared with the recent share price of roughly $4.82, this implies the stock is about 68.0% undervalued on this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Snap is undervalued by 68.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Snap.

Approach 2: Snap Price vs Sales

For companies that are not consistently profitable, P/S is often a more practical yardstick than P/E because it focuses on what the business is generating in revenue rather than earnings that can swing with one off items or heavy investment.

In general, the higher a company’s expected growth and the lower its perceived risk, the higher the P/S multiple investors are usually willing to pay, while slower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower “normal” multiple.

Snap currently trades on a P/S of about 1.37x, compared with the Interactive Media and Services industry average of around 1.00x and a peer group average of roughly 2.11x. Simply Wall St’s proprietary Fair Ratio for Snap is 1.90x, which reflects factors such as earnings growth outlook, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it adjusts for how Snap’s own growth profile, risk level and business mix differ from others rather than assuming all companies should share the same multiple.

With the current P/S of 1.37x sitting below the Fair Ratio of 1.90x, the shares appear undervalued on this metric.

data.title: TotalEnergies Faces Middle East Shock As Output And Cash Flow Shift
data.description:
• TotalEnergies (ENXTPA:TTE) has shut its Satorp refinery in Saudi Arabia due to war-related damage.
  • The company is halting production in Qatar, Iraq, and offshore UAE, affecting Middle East operations.
  • Management indicates around 15% of overall output and 10% of upstream cash flow are currently impacted.

TotalEnergies is a large integrated energy group with a mix of upstream production, refining, and growing low carbon activities. The Middle East is an important hub for its oil and gas operations, so a multi country shutdown of this scale is a material operational event. For investors, it changes where a meaningful portion of volumes and cash generation currently sit.

Attention is likely to stay on how long these Middle East disruptions last and what that means for group level production and cash flow mix. Readers may want to watch for company updates on repair timelines, insurance coverage, and any reallocation of capital spending across regions or business lines.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for TotalEnergies by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on TotalEnergies.

We've flagged 2 risks for TotalEnergies. See which could impact your investment.

The Middle East shutdowns concentrate attention on how exposed TotalEnergies is to region specific shocks and how quickly it can lean on other parts of the portfolio. Around 15% of total output and 10% of upstream cash flow are affected, which is meaningful for an integrated major but still leaves most production and cash generation intact. For readers, the key question is how the company balances short term disruption to hydrocarbon assets with ongoing build out of gas, power and renewables, including the planned $2.2b onshore renewables joint venture in Asia with Masdar. That structure, with 3 GW of operating assets and a larger pipeline to be developed, points to a business model that relies less on any single region or asset over time, but it also increases execution demands across many countries.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The TotalEnergies Narrative

  • The disruption in Qatar, Iraq, UAE and Saudi Arabia lines up with the narrative focus on diversifying away from higher risk regions and scaling LNG, power and renewables to support more stable long term cash flows.
  • The refinery and upstream outages highlight the geopolitical exposure flagged in the narrative as a key threat to profitability, especially where assets sit in high tax or higher cost jurisdictions.
  • The current narrative weighs energy transition projects and divestments, but may not fully reflect the operational and insurance outcomes of a multi country shutdown of 15% of output and how that might change future capital allocation.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for TotalEnergies to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentrated exposure to higher risk regions, including the Middle East, increases the chance of operational disruption or stranded capital if conflicts or regulations intensify.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have already flagged concerns about downstream and petrochemicals margins, and a prolonged Satorp shutdown could add pressure to that part of the business mix.
  • 🎁 The company holds assets outside the affected countries and has been reallocating capital into LNG, renewables and integrated power, which can help reduce reliance on any single region over time.
  • 🎁 Existing narratives point to earnings growth and what is described as good value, suggesting that some geopolitical and transition risks are already considered in current expectations.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on the duration and scope of the Satorp shutdown and the status of production in Qatar, Iraq and offshore UAE, including any guidance on how quickly volumes can return. Follow management commentary on insurance recoveries, potential refinery repairs or upgrades, and whether capital spending is redirected to other upstream, LNG or power projects. It is also worth tracking how this event is reflected in future risk assessments and analyst narratives compared with competitors such as Shell, BP and Exxon Mobil that have their own regional exposure profiles.
